label: test_coverage_gap
summary: The core sliding-window algorithm is correct: timestamps are filtered to the active window before counting, the push-then-compare order means exactly `limit` requests are allowed and the next is rejected with 429, and X-Forwarded-For extraction follows the leftmost-IP convention. However, two acceptance criteria are unmet and block merge. AC5 (cleanup to prevent memory leak) is absent: the `hits` Map grows by one key per unique IP seen and those keys are never evicted, which will exhaust process memory on any long-running server with diverse client traffic. AC6 (test coverage) is critically thin — only the under-limit happy path is tested; the at-limit boundary, 429 rejection path, XFF behavior, window expiry, and cleanup are all unverified. The XFF spoofing risk is a meaningful security note that warrants at minimum a code comment documenting the trust assumption. Both blocking issues are straightforward to fix without redesigning the implementation.

findings.description: The `hits` Map accumulates one entry per unique IP and never removes them; `hits.set(ip, recent)` updates the timestamp array on every request but `hits.delete(ip)` is never called, so the Map grows without bound over the server's lifetime.
findings.suggestion: After filtering, if `recent.length === 0` call `hits.delete(ip)` (lazy eviction), or add a `setInterval` that iterates the Map and deletes entries whose most-recent timestamp is older than `windowMs`.

findings.description: Only one of six required test scenarios is present (under-limit happy path); at-limit boundary, over-limit 429, X-Forwarded-For header, window expiry, and cleanup are entirely absent.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for: (a) exactly `limit` requests all pass with no 429, (b) request `limit+1` returns 429, (c) `x-forwarded-for` header is used instead of `req.ip`, (d) a timestamp older than `windowMs` is not counted so a request after expiry is allowed, (e) expired IP entries are removed from the Map.

findings.description: Using `x-forwarded-for.split(',')[0]` extracts the leftmost IP, which is client-supplied and trivially spoofable; an attacker can bypass per-IP rate limiting by rotating fabricated `X-Forwarded-For` values.
findings.suggestion: Document the trust assumption in a comment; if a trusted proxy is guaranteed to strip client-supplied XFF before appending its own, state that explicitly, otherwise use `req.socket.remoteAddress` or a configurable trusted-proxy count.

findings.description: Middleware parameters `req`, `res`, and `next` are implicitly typed as `any`; in strict TypeScript this produces errors and removes type safety.
findings.suggestion: Type them as `(req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction)` imported from the `express` package.

fullReview.summary: The core sliding-window algorithm is correct: timestamps are filtered to the active window before counting, the push-then-compare order means exactly `limit` requests are allowed and the next is rejected with 429, and X-Forwarded-For extraction follows the leftmost-IP convention. However, two acceptance criteria are unmet and block merge. AC5 (cleanup to prevent memory leak) is absent: the `hits` Map grows by one key per unique IP seen and those keys are never evicted, which will exhaust process memory on any long-running server with diverse client traffic. AC6 (test coverage) is critically thin — only the under-limit happy path is tested; the at-limit boundary, 429 rejection path, XFF behavior, window expiry, and cleanup are all unverified. The XFF spoofing risk is a meaningful security note that warrants at minimum a code comment documenting the trust assumption. Both blocking issues are straightforward to fix without redesigning the implementation.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Middleware rejects with 429 when limit exceeded
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Line 15: `if (recent.length > limit) { return res.status(429).json({ error: 'Too many requests' }) }`. Timestamp is pushed before the check, so the (limit+1)th request in the window has `recent.length === limit+1 > limit` and is correctly rejected.
Lines 10-13: `timestamps.filter((t) => t > now - windowMs)` discards entries outside the window on every request; state is stored per-IP in the `hits` Map keyed by extracted IP string.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Default limit: 100/minute, configurable via middleware options
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Lines 3-4: `opts.limit ?? 100` and `opts.windowMs ?? 60_000`; both fields are exposed on the `RateLimitOptions` interface, allowing callers to override either.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Correctly handles X-Forwarded-For header when behind proxy
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Line 9: `req.headers['x-forwarded-for']?.split(',')[0] ?? req.ip` extracts the first IP from XFF and falls back to `req.ip`. This is the standard leftmost-IP convention. A spoofing warning is filed separately — the criterion as written is satisfied.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Cleans up expired entries to prevent memory leak
criteriaAssessment.evidence: `hits.delete(ip)` is never called anywhere in the file. The array stored per IP is filtered on access, but the Map key itself persists forever. A server that sees N distinct client IPs will hold N Map entries indefinitely regardless of inactivity.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Unit tests cover: under-limit, at-limit, over-limit, proxy header, cleanup
criteriaAssessment.evidence: The test file contains exactly one test (`allows requests under limit`). Tests for at-limit boundary, over-limit 429 assertion, X-Forwarded-For header routing, window expiry, and Map cleanup are all missing.
